scenario: Beacon Telco is reviewing a Camunda 8.8 model with 16 elements and needs to standardise where input mappings can be defined across the diagrams. The team has a documentation cleanup window of 30 days before the model is published to all internal teams for reuse in 2026 projects.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 element types are documented as supporting input mappings in the variables guide?

options.text: Service tasks, script tasks, business rule tasks, call activities, user tasks, send tasks, and subprocesses are the documented element types that support input mappings in Camunda 8.8.
Service tasks, receive tasks, message catch events, and subprocesses are the documented element types that support input mappings in Camunda 8.8 according to the variables guide today.
Start events of a process or subprocess, end events of a process, and intermediate timer events are the documented element types that support input mappings in Camunda 8.8 today.
Only service tasks, user tasks, and call activities are documented as supporting input mappings; subprocesses use output mappings exclusively for variable creation in Camunda 8.8 models.

a.text: Correct. The docs list service tasks, script tasks, business rule tasks, call activities, user tasks, send tasks, and subprocesses as the elements where input mappings can be defined to create local variables.

b.text: Incorrect. That list of receive tasks and message catch events corresponds to the output-mapping element types; input mappings include script tasks, business rule tasks, send tasks, and others.

c.text: Incorrect. Start events, end events, and timer events are not in the documented input-mapping element list for Camunda 8.8 and cannot be used to define input mappings on their own.

d.text: Incorrect. The input-mapping element list includes more element types than these three, and subprocesses support both input and output mappings, not only output mappings as suggested.

explanation: Input mappings can be defined on service tasks, script tasks, business rule tasks, call activities, user tasks, send tasks, and subprocesses. When applied, they create a new local variable in the scope where the mapping is defined, even if the source expression resolves to null.
